skill.name: generate-image
skill.description: Generate and edit general-purpose images through OpenRouter image models such as Gemini and FLUX. Use it for photos, illustrations, artwork, concept visuals, and presentation assets rather than technical diagrams.
skill.summary: Generate and edit AI images through OpenRouter using Gemini and FLUX models.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The critical static findings are false positives after review: the network, base64, and API key patterns support the documented OpenRouter image workflow. The skill still has medium marketplace risk because it reads an OpenRouter key, sends prompts and optional local input images to a third-party API, and writes output files to user-selected paths.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Transmission of User Content
medium_findings.description: The script sends the user prompt and, during editing, a base64-encoded input image to the OpenRouter chat completions endpoint. This is expected for the skill, but users must understand that image content and prompts leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly constructs a multimodal request and posts it to OpenRouter. The behavior is documented and functional, so confidence is high for privacy risk and low for malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: API Key Discovery and Command-Line Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The script searches the current directory and all parent directories for a .env file containing OPENROUTER_API_KEY and also accepts an API key as a command-line argument. This is legitimate authentication, but parent-directory discovery and CLI secrets can expose or use credentials unintentionally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential handling is explicit and tied to OpenRouter authentication. The remaining concern is operational exposure, not evidence of secret theft.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled File Read and Write Paths
medium_findings.description: The --input path is read, encoded, and sent for image editing, while --output controls where decoded image data is written. This supports the stated feature, but it can process sensitive local files if a user or upstream prompt selects the wrong path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and easy to verify. They are user-controlled and documented, which lowers malicious confidence but keeps the data-handling risk meaningful.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings in SKILL.md are fenced or inline Markdown examples that show users how to run the script. They are documentation text, not code that the skill executes automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visibly Markdown usage examples. No runtime parser or executor consumes these examples in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Base64 and Weak Crypto Findings Are Image Encoding
low_findings.description: The base64 operations encode input images and decode image responses for API transport. The weak cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by words such as key or by base64 handling, not by cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses base64 only for image serialization. I found no hashing, encryption, weak cipher selection, or custom cryptographic logic.

low_findings.title: Evaluation Artifact Findings Are Non-Executable
low_findings.description: Several static findings point to evaluation.json, which contains a prior audit report and marketplace content rather than the executable skill. Those entries do not create runtime behavior by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is structured as JSON audit metadata and contains no executable Python or shell code. It can still misstate risk, but it is not an execution path in this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Bearing Network Request
dangerous_patterns.description: The script places the OpenRouter API key in an Authorization header when calling the OpenRouter endpoint. This is required for the tool, but it is a sensitive network pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Authorization header and endpoint are explicit in the reviewed code. The destination matches the documented provider, so the risk is controlled but real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parent Directory .env Search
dangerous_patterns.description: The API key lookup walks from the current working directory through parent directories looking for .env. This can select a broader project or home-level credential without the user naming it directly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop over current_dir.parents is direct evidence. The impact depends on where the user runs the script, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for exploitability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Image Upload to External Model Provider
dangerous_patterns.description: When --input is provided, the script reads the local image, converts it to a data URL, and includes it in the external model request. This may disclose image contents to OpenRouter and the selected model provider.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from file read to request body is visible in the code. It is expected for image editing but should be disclosed to users.

content.value_statement: Teams need visual assets without leaving their AI workflow. This skill generates and edits images through OpenRouter models using direct prompts and local files.
actual_capabilities: Generate images from text prompts using OpenRouter image models.
Edit existing PNG, JPEG, GIF, or WebP images with text instructions.
Select Gemini or FLUX model IDs through a command-line option.
Save returned image data as a PNG file at a chosen output path.
Load an OpenRouter API key from .env or a command-line argument.
limitations: Requires a valid OpenRouter API key and available provider credits.
Sends prompts and edited input images to an external API provider.
Designed for general images, not technical diagrams or schematics.
Does not validate model pricing, provider policy, or generated content quality.

use_cases.title: Create Presentation Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate slide backgrounds, hero images, and conceptual visuals for talks or internal documents.

use_cases.title: Produce Research Illustrations
use_cases.description: Create non-schematic scientific illustrations for papers, posters, and explanatory material.

use_cases.title: Edit Draft Image Assets
use_cases.description: Apply prompt-based changes to local images before using them in articles, websites, or campaigns.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an image of [subject] in [style]. Use [mood], [lighting], and [composition details].

prompt_templates.title: Presentation Asset
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a polished visual for slides
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a presentation visual showing [concept]. Make it clean, professional, and suitable for a [audience] slide deck.

prompt_templates.title: Edit Existing Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit [input image path] by changing [specific element] to [desired result]. Preserve [parts that must stay unchanged].

prompt_templates.title: Model-Controlled Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced generation with model and output choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate [detailed visual brief] using [Gemini or FLUX model ID]. Save the result to [output filename].

output_examples.input: Generate a scientific illustration of immune cells surrounding a tumor cell for a conference slide.
output_examples.output: Creates a polished conceptual image and saves it as generated_image.png for slide use.

output_examples.input: Edit portrait.png to make the background a soft studio blue while preserving the person.
output_examples.output: Reads the local portrait image, sends the edit request to OpenRouter, and writes an edited PNG file.

output_examples.input: Generate an abstract hero image for an AI research report using a FLUX model.
output_examples.output: Uses the requested model and saves a new visual asset at the selected output path.

best_practices: Review prompts and input images before sending them to OpenRouter.
Store the API key in a project-specific .env file with restricted access.
Use explicit output paths so generated images are saved in the intended directory.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for confidential images without provider approval.
Do not pass API keys in shared command histories or prompts.
Do not use this skill for flowcharts, circuits, or precise technical diagrams.

faq.question: What API key does this skill require?
faq.answer: It requires an OpenRouter API key stored in .env or passed as an argument.

faq.question: Does the skill send data outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. It sends prompts and optional input images to the OpenRouter API.

faq.question: Can it edit existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide an input image path and describe the edits in the prompt.

faq.question: Which input image formats are supported?
faq.answer: The script recognizes PNG, JPEG, GIF, and WebP input files for editing.

faq.question: Is it safe for technical diagrams?
faq.answer: No. It is intended for general images and illustrations, not precise schematics.

faq.question: Where are generated images saved?
faq.answer: The script saves output as a PNG file at the default or specified output path.
